the_mechanism.what_actually_happens: For a list, reconciler builds a key->fiber map of old children. Iterates new children: if key matches an old fiber, reuse it (just update props, possibly move DOM node). If no match, mount new. Unmatched old fibers are unmounted. For move operations, React uses lastPlacedIndex tracking to determine if a node needs DOM move (appendChild).
the_mechanism.what_makes_it_efficient: Keyed reconciliation is O(n) with hash lookup. DOM moves (insertBefore) cheaper than full unmount+mount. Component state preserved across reorders.

common_misunderstandings.misconception: Any unique value works as key
common_misunderstandings.reality: Must be STABLE across renders. Math.random() or generated each render means new identity each time
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Components remount every render, state lost, animations restart

common_misunderstandings.misconception: Index keys are fine for static lists
common_misunderstandings.reality: True - if list never reorders/inserts/deletes, index = identity
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Devs use index keys, then add sort/filter, everything breaks subtly

common_misunderstandings.misconception: Keys need to be globally unique
common_misunderstandings.reality: Only unique among siblings
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Devs over-engineer key generation

when_this_is_wrong_tool.better_approach: Index keys are fine - identity doesn't matter
Use item IDs - virtual scrolling reorders DOM constantly

what_deep_understanding_enables: You design list rendering with stable identity. You use key as a remount signal intentionally. You diagnose 'why does my input lose focus on reorder' by checking keys. You understand why react-beautiful-dnd / dnd-kit require key consistency.

concept: Selector-based subscriptions vs context propagation
naive_understanding: Juniors don't see a difference between context and Zustand/Redux - they treat all global state the same and are surprised by performance differences.

the_problem_space.before_this_concept_existed: Pre-Redux/Zustand era, global state lived in module globals or jQuery's $.data. No reactivity. With React, Context was the only built-in option, but it re-rendered all consumers on value change.
specific_pain_points: A counter increment caused a 100-item list to re-render
Different parts of UI care about different fields but pay rerender cost for unrelated changes
Memoization via useMemo for context values still triggered subscribers
the_problem_space.constraints_that_shaped_solution: Need granular subscriptions: 'tell me when ONLY this field changes'. React's context is coarse - subscribe to the whole object. External stores can subscribe per-selector if integrated correctly with React's render cycle.

failed_solutions.why_it_failed: Combinatorial explosion - 50 contexts is unwieldy
Consumer still re-renders on context change - just doesn't re-derive

the_core_insight: True selector-based state needs an external store with its own subscription model. Components subscribe to a SELECTOR function. Store change triggers all subscribers, each computes its selector, compares to previous result, and triggers React re-render only if different. useSyncExternalStore is the React 18+ hook that integrates this with concurrent rendering safely (no tearing).

the_mechanism.what_actually_happens: Store maintains state + Set of listeners. On state update, calls all listeners. Each component's useSyncExternalStore listener computes selector(state), compares with previous, calls forceUpdate if changed. useSyncExternalStore takes (subscribe, getSnapshot, getServerSnapshot) - it handles tear-prevention by re-reading snapshot synchronously in concurrent renders.
the_mechanism.what_makes_it_efficient: Subscription is per-selector. Reference equality on selector output skips unchanged consumers. No tree-walking - flat listener set. Concurrent-safe via useSyncExternalStore.

common_misunderstandings.misconception: Zustand is faster than Context
common_misunderstandings.reality: It's the SELECTOR SUBSCRIPTION model that's faster - not the library itself
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Devs think state management is magic; same overhead applies if used naively (subscribing to entire store)

common_misunderstandings.misconception: useSyncExternalStore is just for libraries
common_misunderstandings.reality: It's a stable primitive for any external state integration
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Devs build custom hooks with useState + useEffect for external stores and hit tearing bugs

common_misunderstandings.misconception: Tearing doesn't happen in normal apps
common_misunderstandings.reality: Concurrent rendering can split render across time - external state read during render in different fibers can differ
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Subtle bugs with derived data inconsistent within one frame

when_this_is_wrong_tool.scenario: State scoped to a feature tree
when_this_is_wrong_tool.better_approach: Local state + context - global store is overkill
React Query / SWR - they handle caching, revalidation, optimistic updates

what_deep_understanding_enables: You pick the right state tool: context for stable config, external store for hot mutable state, React Query for server state. You debug perf by tracing which selectors fire. You build custom external state integrations with useSyncExternalStore.

concept: Reflow, repaint, and the dirty rect
naive_understanding: Juniors group all 'slow CSS' together. They don't distinguish layout-triggering changes (reflow whole document) from paint-only changes (repaint a region) from composite-only.

the_problem_space.before_this_concept_existed: Early browsers redrew everything on any change. CRT-era page redraws were visible. As pages grew, full reflow on every keystroke became prohibitive.
specific_pain_points: Setting element.style.width in a loop reflowed N times
Reading offsetWidth after writing forced sync layout (layout thrashing)
Hover styles on a tiny button repainted the whole page
the_problem_space.constraints_that_shaped_solution: Layout is interdependent - changing one element's width can affect parents (auto sizing), siblings (flex/grid), descendants (text wrap). Browsers must compute affected subtree, not just the changed element.

failed_solutions.why_it_failed: Cache invalidation impossible to maintain
Easy to break - one stray read forces flush

the_core_insight: Browser does work LAZILY and BATCHED. Writes mark things dirty; reads force flush. The pipeline: invalidate -> recalc style -> layout -> paint -> composite. Each step has a 'dirty' set. Layout thrashing happens when you alternate read/write because each read forces synchronous layout. Compositing only changes (transform/opacity on layer) skip layout AND paint entirely.

the_mechanism.what_actually_happens: Style change -> invalidate computed style for affected elements. Read forces recalc + layout flush. Paint operates on damage rect (bounding box of changed pixels). For a layer-promoted element, paint is to its texture; composite combines layers via GPU. Forced sync layout (FSL) is the costly anti-pattern: write A, read B (flushes), write C, read D (flushes again).
the_mechanism.what_makes_it_efficient: Lazy batched pipeline means writes are cheap (just invalidate) until forced flush. Damage tracking limits paint to changed regions. Compositing offloads to GPU thread.

common_misunderstandings.misconception: display: none is free
common_misunderstandings.reality: Removing from layout invalidates parent layout; toggling causes reflow
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Surprised when show/hide loops are slow

common_misunderstandings.misconception: Class toggling is cheaper than style changes
common_misunderstandings.reality: Same pipeline - depends on which properties change
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Adding class with width change has same cost as direct style.width

common_misunderstandings.misconception: requestAnimationFrame avoids layout thrashing
common_misunderstandings.reality: It schedules to next frame but reads in the callback still flush
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Devs put thrashing code in rAF thinking they're safe

when_this_is_wrong_tool.scenario: Need exact pixel measurements during animation
when_this_is_wrong_tool.better_approach: Use IntersectionObserver/ResizeObserver - async, no FSL
Animate via transform, not width/height

what_deep_understanding_enables: You avoid layout thrashing by batching. You categorize CSS changes by pipeline impact. You use Paint Flashing and Layer borders devtools. You design animations for composite-only paths. You leverage ResizeObserver/IntersectionObserver for async measurement.

concept: Optimistic UI and reconciling local with server state
naive_understanding: Juniors implement 'optimistic' as 'just setState before await fetch' - missing the rollback, conflict, and intermediate-state complexity.

the_problem_space.before_this_concept_existed: Traditional submit: button click -> spinner -> wait for server -> update UI. Felt slow. Even with 100ms server, the perception was 'app is slow' due to mandatory wait.
specific_pain_points: Submit button felt unresponsive even on fast networks
Like button required round-trip before showing liked state
Bulk actions had user staring at spinners
the_problem_space.constraints_that_shaped_solution: User perception of speed is dominated by first-paint after click (INP). Server response is necessary for truth but not for perceived response. Server might fail, conflict, or return modified data.

failed_solutions.why_it_failed: On failure, UI shows success state; phantom data persists
Slow perceived response

the_core_insight: UI state has two roles: REFLECT server truth, and PREDICT user intent. Optimistic UI predicts assuming success, then reconciles with reality. Two states: 'pending' (predicted, awaiting confirmation) and 'committed' (server-confirmed). On success, replace prediction with truth. On failure, rollback or surface error. React 19's useOptimistic hook formalizes this.

the_mechanism.what_actually_happens: User action triggers (1) optimistic state update (visible immediately), (2) async server request. While pending, UI shows optimistic state. On response: if success, merge server response into committed state and remove optimistic overlay. If failure, remove optimistic overlay, show error, and depending on UX either revert visibly or keep the failed input for user retry.
the_mechanism.what_makes_it_efficient: Perceived 0ms response time. Server roundtrip happens in background. User stays in flow.

common_misunderstandings.misconception: Optimistic UI means lying to the user
common_misunderstandings.reality: It's predictive based on high success probability + correction on failure
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Devs avoid optimistic UI thinking it's deceptive; users get worse UX

common_misunderstandings.misconception: Just setState before await is enough
common_misunderstandings.reality: Need rollback path, conflict resolution, server-truth reconciliation
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Phantom data on failures, double-counted on retries

common_misunderstandings.misconception: Optimistic updates work for all operations
common_misunderstandings.reality: Predict-and-correct works when failure is rare AND rollback is acceptable. Not for irreversible actions
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Optimistic 'send email' would be confusing if failure means email actually sent or not

when_this_is_wrong_tool.scenario: Operations with significant server-side computation result
when_this_is_wrong_tool.better_approach: Show spinner with progress - user expects to wait
Confirmation + wait for server - rollback isn't safe

what_deep_understanding_enables: You design responsive UIs that feel instant. You handle failure with grace (toast, retry). You use useOptimistic for clean rollback. You distinguish actions safe to optimize vs. those needing confirmation.

concept: CSS custom properties and runtime cascade values
naive_understanding: Juniors treat CSS variables as 'Sass variables but in CSS' - missing that they cascade, inherit, are LIVE values readable/writable from JS, and enable dynamic theming impossible with build-time variables.

the_problem_space.before_this_concept_existed: Sass/Less variables were compile-time constants. Theming required generating multiple CSS files (light.css, dark.css) and swapping. Runtime values (current scroll, viewport) couldn't influence styles without inline style props.
specific_pain_points: Theme switching required swapping stylesheets - flash of unstyled content
Dynamic values (mouse position, scroll progress) required inline style which fragmented styling
User-customizable colors needed JS to manipulate stylesheets
the_problem_space.constraints_that_shaped_solution: Want runtime mutability. Want cascade and inheritance. Want JS interop. Must not slow down style recalc significantly.

failed_solutions.why_it_failed: Compile-time only, no JS interop, no runtime change
Specificity 1,0,0,0 (high), no cascade benefit, breaks separation

the_core_insight: CSS Custom Properties are PROPERTIES, not variables. They cascade like color and inherit like font-family. Any element reads --foo from its nearest ancestor that sets it. JS can read/write via getComputedStyle/style.setProperty. Combined with calc() and color-mix(), they enable runtime-computed dynamic styling without re-rendering or stylesheet swapping.

the_mechanism.what_actually_happens: --foo: value declared on an element becomes part of its computed style. Descendants reading var(--foo) look up the cascade. JS setProperty changes the value, invalidates style/paint/composite for descendants reading it. The browser optimizes by tracking which selectors use which variables. Cascade applies normally - more-specific selectors override.
the_mechanism.what_makes_it_efficient: Theme switching is one property change cascading - no stylesheet swap. JS interop is direct. With @property, typed values enable interpolation animations. Tailwind v4 uses CSS variables for all theming.

common_misunderstandings.misconception: CSS variables are slow
common_misunderstandings.reality: Negligible overhead - they're computed once per cascade
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Devs avoid them for perf reasons, lose theming flexibility

common_misunderstandings.misconception: --foo: 10 is a number
common_misunderstandings.reality: All custom property values are TOKEN strings unless typed with @property
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: calc(var(--foo) * 2) works because calc parses; bare arithmetic doesn't

common_misunderstandings.misconception: Custom properties don't inherit
common_misunderstandings.reality: They DO inherit by default - that's why they cascade
common_misunderstandings.consequences_of_misunderstanding: Devs redeclare on every nested element unnecessarily

when_this_is_wrong_tool.scenario: Pure logic-driven values not visible in CSS
when_this_is_wrong_tool.better_approach: Keep in JS state - don't round-trip through DOM
Sass variables - custom properties are not in IE

what_deep_understanding_enables: You build runtime theming without stylesheet swaps. You leverage @property for animatable typed values. You bridge JS and CSS with setProperty for scroll/mouse-driven effects. You understand Tailwind v4's theme architecture. You stop reaching for inline styles when CSS variables are cleaner.
